Collection › Song Dynasty › ro-song-007 — Guan Yao Ribbed-Neck Vase

Reference Object ro-song-007

Guan Yao Ribbed-Neck Vase with Grey-Green Celadon Glaze

官窑 长颈瓶 — Southern Song Dynasty, 12th–13th century CE

Southern Song Guan Ware Grey-Green Celadon Zi Kou Tie Zu Bingliewen Glaze Pooling Phase Separation Dust-Matte Texture GOMP-2025

Object Description

A ribbed-neck globular vase of the Bangchui Ping tradition (棒槌瓶), combining a compressed globular body, a tall cylindrical neck articulated by three horizontal wheel-thrown ribs, and a trumpet-shaped mouth. The entire surface is covered with an exceptionally thick grey-green celadon glaze applied in multiple layers. Visual emphasis is created exclusively by silhouette and glaze in keeping with Southern Song imperial monochrome aesthetics.

The glaze is soft grey-green with a slight bluish modulation, silky surface, gentle opalescence, and pronounced translucent depth. The shoulder preserves a glaze-pooling ridge produced during firing — one of the defining characteristics of Guan ware. The rim shows the classic Zi Kou (紫口, Purple Mouth) effect: slight purplish-grey darkening at the thinned glaze edge visible under raking light. The foot ring displays the Tie Zu (铁足, Iron Foot): a narrow exposed ring revealing the dark iron-rich body, with the glazed base interior showing a continuous crackle network.

Microscopic Diagnostic Analysis

The vase was examined using the Dronova Gemmological Optical Microscopy Protocol (GOMP-2025) under reflected and oblique illumination at magnifications from 20× to 100×. The investigation focused on glaze surface microtexture, crackle morphology, the Zi Kou / Tie Zu complex, glaze pooling, and internal glaze microstructure including microbubbles, phase separation, and crystalline formations.

The silky appearance of this glaze, observed macroscopically, originates from the complex internal microstructure: thick calcium-alkaline glaze → bubble-rich structure → phase separation → crystal-related light scattering → jade-like optical depth. The visual appearance directly reflects original kiln technology.

GOM Documentation — Crystalline Dust-Matte Surface Texture

~40×

Fig. 5. Crystalline Dust-Matte Texture — the hallmark diagnostic surface trait of authentic Guan yao glaze. Fine granular surface relief with gentle uniform scattering, absent from modern reproductions which present either a fully glassy surface or coarser artificial granulation.

~60×

Fig. 6. Glaze microstructure at intermediate magnification. Phase-separated domains visible as lighter and darker zones within the glaze matrix. Microbubble distribution contributes to internal light scattering producing the opalescent jade-like quality.

GOM Documentation — Crystalline Microstructure at High Magnification

~100×

Fig. 7. Crystalline microstructure at high magnification. Crystal aggregates, phase-separation structures, and microbubble remnants are visible within the glaze matrix. This complex microstructure, formed during slow cooling at approximately 1220°C, is the optical basis of the characteristic Guan ware glaze appearance.

1. Crystalline Dust-Matte Glaze Texture

The most immediately diagnostic feature of this glaze is its characteristic crystalline dust-matte surface texture, visible across all glazed surfaces under both direct and oblique illumination. This texture is produced by a population of microcrystals and phase-separated domains near the glaze surface that scatter incident light uniformly rather than reflecting it specularly. The texture is absent from both modern reproductions (which present either a fully glassy or coarser artificial surface) and from later dynasty imitations of Guan ware, making it one of the most reliable visual diagnostic indicators accessible without magnification.

2. Bingliewen Ice Crackle — 冰裂纹

The glaze is covered by a uniform fine-to-medium crackle network (冰裂纹, bingliewen) extending continuously across all surfaces including the body, neck, mouth, and glazed base interior. The crack channels remain only lightly coloured, producing the refined appearance characteristic of Guan ware rather than the strongly contrasting crack lines of Ge ware. The continuous and morphologically consistent propagation of the crackle network across all surfaces confirms original glaze formation. Crack penetration through the full glaze thickness is consistent with tensile stress during cooling of a thick calcium-alkaline glaze with higher thermal expansion than the iron-rich body.

3. Zi Kou Tie Zu Complex — 紫口铁足

The classical Southern Song Guan diagnostic complex is clearly present. The Zi Kou (紫口, Purple Mouth) effect presents as a slight purplish-grey darkening at the thinned glaze rim, visible under raking light, produced by the iron-rich body showing through the reduced glaze thickness at the rim edge. The Tie Zu (铁足, Iron Foot) comprises a narrow unglazed foot ring exposing the dark iron-rich stoneware body, with the glazed base centre carrying the continuous crackle pattern. This combination remains one of the strongest visual indicators of authentic Southern Song Guan production.

4. Glaze Pooling

A pronounced glaze-pooling ridge is preserved at the neck–body junction on the shoulder. This accumulation of glaze material records the direction of gravitational flow during firing and can only be produced during the original high-temperature firing event when the glaze was sufficiently fluid. It cannot be reproduced by post-firing surface treatment. The pooling ridge also confirms the multi-layer glaze application: each successive layer contributed to the eventual pooled accumulation.

5. Multi-Layer Glaze Microstructure and Phase Separation

High-magnification GOM examination reveals abundant microbubbles, granular surface texture, phase separation domains, crystal-related light scattering, and a diffuse glaze–body interface with iron migration. These features collectively confirm the characteristic microstructure of Southern Song Guan ware: a thick calcium-alkaline glaze fired in a reducing atmosphere at approximately 1220°C, producing through slow cooling the bubble-rich, phase-separated internal structure responsible for the jade-like optical depth observed macroscopically.

Diagnostic Interpretation

The vase demonstrates complete technological consistency. Every observed characteristic agrees with published archaeological and materials-science investigations of Southern Song Guan ware. No conflicting features were identified. The agreement between morphology, glaze chemistry, crackle development, glaze–body interaction, firing technology, and museum comparanda forms a coherent diagnostic system.

Key Diagnostic Markers

Crystalline Dust-Matte glaze texture — hallmark of authentic Guan yao
Bingliewen ice crackle (冰裂纹) — continuous across all surfaces
Zi Kou (紫口) — purplish-grey rim darkening under raking light
Tie Zu (铁足) — dark iron-rich foot ring, no spur marks
Glaze pooling ridge at neck–body junction
Multi-layer thick grey-green celadon glaze
Jade-like optical depth with opalescence
Phase separation and microbubble population
No restoration detected
High internal diagnostic consistency

GOMP-2025 Score: 89 / 100 — High Attribution Confidence
